publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 132-134. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: St James' Sqre Thursdy Morg 5th Decr
Dearest Mate. How sensible are your letters always.--

I am sorry that owing to the Hen's blunder as to the time (of the Traitor's visit to Ockham), you have thus had the trouble of making out a useless list of visitors. It was in Jany last, as I wrote you yesterday, & is believed to have been rather early in that month than otherwise.
A friend of my mother's & of ours dined at Murray's & heard there as public news the report of this base-minded visitor; but could not discover

the name of the person, nor even the sex. Only that every one had believed the report, because the informant stated that he (or she) had just come from Ockham & knew it all for positive fact. It must have been a man I think without doubt. Our friend did not wish the precise words of the report to go to you, & would be annoyed at the idea of it.

I am therefore obliged to with-hold this degree of exact information. I was told, but not at first, & with reluctance. It is a most base & scandalous insinuation, tho' of a general nature. The reporter gave no facts. Indeed he could not, unless he had absolutely invented them.

Now it is really of importance to detect who it could be; for one ought not to harbour a Serpent unconsciously in one's household; one who will fabricate; or at any rate maliciously pervert every real incident.
I am really very indignant; not as regards us; for he can do us but little harm in the end; but at such abstract baseness in itself considered.

If we can actually find out the who, my Mother & I have a plan for something I can do respecting him; which will be a terrible lesson to the party. The opportunity for it may not occur perhaps for a long time; but if so, then so much the more striking to him the
retribution. It is a thing which no one but myself perhaps could do; requiring great temper, great dignity, great force of character too.

My mother you know is never by way of noticing every nonsense that silly people say of one. But she does think this a most malicious degree of baseness, & she that should be fathomed & if possible noticed.--
You will see from the enclosed from her that I had utterly mistaken the import of her "note of admiration!" about C. B's marriage. I am grievously disappointed & anxious, about it.--
What a fool Miss Cooper is. I have had sad proofs of this now.
I got the Athenaem yesterday morning. I do not think Miss M's letters well-judged for the public, & I cannot say that they carry absolute conviction to me.
I am well; decidedly making great progress. This is simply I believe from the persevering attention to rigid diet.
I truly believe that in time I shall have excellent health by this means. So does the Hen.
But never must I forget the past, in that case.
